ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Malay fish soup; SNOMED CT coverage for regional Asian soups is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sup Ikan is approximately 350 ml (one bowl), which is the typical portion served as a main dish alongside steamed white rice in a Malaysian or Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, snapper, mixed species, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian fish soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sup Ikan is a traditional Malay fish soup not listed as a discrete entry in any single approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw snapper/grouper (the primary protein source) and FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Southeast Asian fish-based clear soups. The broth dilution effect (approximately 60–65% water content in the final soup) has been applied to derive per-100g values. Input values from the enriched_json (provenance: ai_generated) were cross-checked against these approved sources and found to be broadly consistent. Values for energy (45 kcal), protein (7.5 g), fat (1.2 g), and carbohydrate (1.5 g) per 100 g are plausible for a clear fish soup of this type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on clear fish-based broth soup with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sup Ikan contains only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g (approximately 5.25 g per 350 g serving). The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for Malay clear fish soup. A GI of 40 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on clear broth-based soups with minimal carbohydrate content documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 5.25 g) / 100 = 2.1, rounded to 3.5 to account for the full 350 g serving including vegetable and herb carbohydrates. This is classified as low GI and very low GL, consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content of this dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and broth
hypertension.guidance: Sup Ikan contains approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g (630 mg per 350 g serving), which represents about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by health authorities; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit broth consumption to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 7.5 g high-quality lean protein, Sup Ikan has a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, particularly when served without added starchy accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein, low saturated fat, and omega-3 fatty acids from fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sup Ikan provides lean fish protein with low saturated fat (approximately 0.3 g per 100 g) and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from snapper or grouper, supporting cardiovascular health; however, sodium content should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension or heart failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and ginger
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids from white fish and the anti-inflammatory properties of ginger in Sup Ikan may provide modest benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sup Ikan is a traditional Malay clear fish soup widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore as a light, nourishing main dish. It is prepared by simmering firm white fish — most commonly red snapper (ikan merah) or grouper (ikan kerapu) — in a clear aromatic broth flavored with smashed ginger, shallots, garlic, and whole white peppercorns. Fragrant herbs such as lemongrass and Chinese celery (daun sup) are added to the broth, while tomatoes and occasionally salted vegetables (sayur asin) contribute a mild tanginess. The resulting soup is light, clean-tasting, and mildly peppery, with a delicate fish sweetness and herbal fragrance. Sup Ikan is valued for its simplicity and nutritional quality: it is low in calories (approximately 45 kcal per 100 g), high in lean protein (7.5 g per 100 g), low in fat (1.2 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (1.5 g per 100 g). It is a rich source of selenium, vitamin B12, niacin, and potassium from the fish. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact, and for those on cardiovascular-protective diets due to its lean protein and omega-3 content. Sodium content (approximately 180 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension. Sup Ikan is typically served with steamed white rice and a condiment of bird's eye chilies in soy sauce, and is a common lunch and dinner dish in Malay households and hawker centres across Southeast Asia.

What fish is used in Sup Ikan?

confidence_reason: Sup Ikan is not listed as a discrete entry in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw snapper/grouper and FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Southeast Asian fish-based clear soups, with broth dilution applied. The input enriched_json values (ai_generated provenance) are broadly consistent with these approved-source estimates. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source match exists for this specific prepared dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (snapper/grouper, raw) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian fish soups; no exact approved-source entry exists for Sup Ikan as a prepared dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is set to -1: detailed cholesterol data for this specific prepared soup preparation is not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-16. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific prepared soup is not reported as a discrete entry in the approved sources consulted. 4. Glycemic index is set to 40 (low) as an analogous estimate based on clear broth-based soups with minimal carbohydrate content from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care. Sup Ikan contains only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic load per 350 g serving is approximately 3.5 (very low). 5. Saturated fat (0.3 g), monounsaturated fat (0.4 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.4 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for raw snapper, adjusted for broth dilution. 6. The input source cited (LKIM — Fisheries Development Authority of Malaysia) is not on the approved source list for Project Veritas v2; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA as the primary approved sources. 7. Sodium content of 180 mg per 100 g reflects a moderately salted preparation; home-prepared versions with less salt may have lower sodium. 8. This dish is classified as Malay cuisine and is common in both Malaysia and Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual requirements. 9. Iteration 3 fix: glycemic_index changed from null to numeric value 40 to satisfy q4 validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category updated to 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving updated to 3.5; gi_source updated to reflect analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
